title: Inspection Procedures: Minimum Requirements
narrationScript: Every penetrant inspection runs on a written procedure, and at Level Two that document stops being something you merely follow — it becomes something you review. This lesson sets out the minimum a procedure must contain: scope, reference documents, personnel, materials called out by type, method, sensitivity level, developer form, and remover class, every processing step with real numbers, examination lighting, acceptance criteria, post-cleaning, records, and approval. ASTM E1417 sets the floor — a written procedure applicable to the part, compliant with the practice, approved by a certified Level Three, and submitted to the cognizant engineering organization when the contract requires it. You will learn the limits a reviewer checks numbers against: the forty to one twenty-five degree Fahrenheit processing window, the forty p s i wash cap, the one sixty degree dryer limit, the thousand microwatt black light minimum. Then you will practice the two-pass review — completeness first, workability second. If any element is missing, vague, or impossible on your floor, the draft goes back. Nobody fixes a procedure at the bench.

sections.heading: The Procedure Is Where Requirements Become Work
sections.body:
A penetrant indication is the end of a long chain of choices — which materials, what dwell, how hard a wash, what light. Change any of them and you change what the part shows. The **written procedure** exists to freeze those choices, so that any qualified inspector, on any shift, processes the part the same way and finds the same flaws.

PT runs on documents in a strict order of authority. The **contract, drawing, or purchase order** invokes the method and the acceptance criteria. An **industry standard practice** — ASTM E1417 for most aerospace work, ASME Code Section V, Article 6 for code work — sets the minimum process requirements. The **employer's program** supplies qualified personnel and qualified materials. The **written procedure** then translates all of it into part-specific instructions: this part, these materials, these numbers, this sequence. You do not inspect "to E1417" — you inspect to a procedure that complies with it.

At Level II the document changes character. A Level I works to it under supervision. You perform, interpret, and evaluate to it — and you are usually the first person asked whether a new draft is complete and workable before it reaches the floor.

sections.caption: The document chain: each tier must comply with every tier above it, and the written procedure is where requirements become part-specific instructions.

sections.heading: The Minimum-Content Rule
sections.body:
Governing practices do not leave procedure content to taste. ASTM E1417 requires penetrant examination to be performed **in accordance with a written procedure applicable to the part or group of parts** under examination, and requires that procedure to comply with the practice itself. Three control points follow:

- **Approval.** The procedure must be approved by an individual qualified and certified as **Level III in PT** — under NAS 410, approving procedures and work instructions for technical adequacy is a defined Level III responsibility. When the contract requires it, the procedure is also submitted to the **cognizant engineering organization (CEO)** — the customer's engineering authority — for review and approval.
- **Revision control.** A procedure is a controlled document: revisions carry the same approvals as the original, and the revision at the bench must be the revision on file. On code work, ASME Section V, Article 6 formalizes this with **essential variables** — change one (penetrant material family, removal method, developer application, minimum light intensity, processing temperature outside the standard range) and the procedure must be revised and, where demonstration is specified, **requalified**. Nonessential variables, such as post-examination cleaning technique, require revision only.
- **Availability.** The governing revision must be in the inspector's hands at the station — not a marked-up photocopy from two revisions ago.

sections.caption: Procedure minimum-content checklist — if any row is missing or vague, the procedure is not ready for the floor.
rows: Scope / applicability
Part numbers or family, alloy, surface condition, where PT falls in the process flow, areas to be examined
Does it cover this part, at this manufacturing point?
Reference documents
Governing practice and acceptance standard, each with revision
Current revisions, consistent with the contract?
Personnel
Certification required to process, and to interpret and evaluate results
Who may sign accept / reject?
Materials
AMS 2644 classification — Type, Method, sensitivity level, developer form, remover class — with QPL listing
Could a stranger pull the right chemicals from stores?
Pre-cleaning / surface preparation
Allowed cleaning methods, etch when required, dryness before penetrant
Will flaw openings actually be clean and open?
Processing temperature
Qualified range — standard practice is 40-125 °F (4-52 °C)
What happens on a cold morning or a hot part?
Penetrant application and dwell
Application method, minimum and maximum dwell, keep-wet rule
Are both ends of the dwell window stated?
Excess penetrant removal
Wash pressure and temperature, emulsifier concentration and contact time, or solvent-wipe technique
Are the numbers inside the governing limits?
Drying / developer
Dryer temperature limit and time; developer form, application, development window
Dry first or develop first — does the order match the form?
Examination conditions
UV-A irradiance and ambient-light limits, or white-light minimum; dark adaptation
Can the booth meet these numbers today?
Evaluation / acceptance criteria
How indications are interpreted and evaluated, and the specific acceptance document or stated limits
Would two inspectors reach the same accept / reject?
Post-clean, records, approval
Post-cleaning and protection, what is recorded and marked, Level III approval block and revision history
Is the evidence trail complete — and is it signed?

sections.caption: Every block on a minimum-content procedure does a job: identification and control at the top and bottom, process definition with real numbers in the middle, and objective decision rules before any part is stamped.

sections.heading: The Numbers a Reviewer Checks
sections.body:
A completeness review is partly an arithmetic audit: every parameter in the draft must sit inside the limits of the governing practice. Checking a draft against ASTM E1417, these are the values to know:

- **Processing temperature:** 40-125 °F (4-52 °C); outside that window only with a documented, approved qualification
- **Penetrant dwell:** minimum 10 minutes, and the penetrant must not be allowed to dry — extended dwells (commonly beyond about 2 hours) require rewetting with penetrant
- **Lipophilic emulsifier (Method B):** contact time no more than 3 minutes
- **Hydrophilic remover (Method D):** immersion concentration no more than 35 % by volume; spray concentration no more than 5 %
- **Water wash:** 50-100 °F (10-38 °C) water, spray pressure no more than 40 psi (275 kPa)
- **Drying:** part surface temperature no more than 160 °F (71 °C), for the minimum effective time
- **Development:** minimum 10 minutes; maximum stated by form — commonly 1 to 4 hours depending on the developer
- **Fluorescent examination:** at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the part surface, ambient white light at or below 2 fc (about 20 lx), and at least 1 minute of dark adaptation — some codes, such as ASME Section V, require 5 minutes
- **Visible examination:** at least 100 fc (about 1,000 lx) of white light at the surface

Minimums and maximums both matter, because the process fails in both directions: a dwell too short starves capillary entry, a dwell left to dry locks penetrant in the flaw; a development time too short gives no bleed-out, too long fuzzes indications past honest evaluation. A draft that lists only minimums is incomplete.

sections.heading: Two Passes: Completeness, Then Workability
sections.body:
Review a draft in two distinct passes, in this order.

**Pass 1 — completeness.** Walk the minimum-content checklist line by line. Every required element present. Every parameter carrying a number, and every number inside the governing practice's limits. References current, consistent with the contract, and specific — "evaluate to applicable standards" identifies nothing and decides nothing. Materials identified by classification, not trade name alone. Approval block correct for the revision.

**Pass 2 — workability.** A procedure can be complete and still impossible. Walk the part through the shop on paper. Can the equipment hold the numbers — is there a regulated wash supply that stays under 40 psi, can the oven hold 160 °F? Does the immersion line fit the part, and will penetrant drain from its pockets? Is the called-out developer form stocked, in date, and on the QPL? Can the booth actually reach 2 fc ambient? Does dwell plus development fit the line's pace without parts queuing past their maximums? Where the contract or quality system requires it, the draft is also **demonstrated** — run on representative hardware or a known-defect standard before release.

A failure on either pass has exactly one outcome: the draft goes back for revision. The one thing a reviewer never does is "fix it at the bench" — an inspector improvising around a bad procedure is an uncontrolled process wearing a controlled one's paperwork.

sections.caption: The two-pass review: a draft earns the floor only after every element is present and in-limits, and the shop can execute it exactly as written.

sections.title: Red flags that fail a draft on sight
sections.body:
- Verbs without numbers: "clean thoroughly," "dry completely," "use adequate light"
- Materials by trade name only — no Type, Method, sensitivity level, form, or class, so no QPL check is possible
- Minimums with no maximums on dwell, development, or time between steps
- Acceptance criteria by vague pointer — no document, no revision, no limits
- Parameters outside the governing practice with no qualification attached
- No Level III approval, or a bench copy that does not match the revision on file

Any one of these means the procedure cannot control the process it describes.

figures.title: Anatomy of a minimum-content PT procedure
The two-pass review before first use

interactive.title: Review the Draft Before It Hits the Floor
interactive.intro: Quality hands you draft procedure PT-204 for a family of machined 7075 aluminum fittings — Type I fluorescent, Method D hydrophilic removal, Form a dry-powder developer, written to ASTM E1417. Production wants it released by Friday. You are the Level II assigned the completeness-and-workability review before it goes to the Level III. Make the calls.

steps.prompt: The materials section reads, in full: "Penetrant: BrightGlow 940. Developer: D-90 powder. Remover: as available." Your call?
options: Acceptable — trade names identify the products precisely enough
Deficient — materials must be identified by AMS 2644 classification (Type, Method, sensitivity level, form, remover class) and be verifiable against the QPL; "as available" controls nothing
Deficient — procedures may never mention trade names
Acceptable once the SDS for each product is attached
steps.feedback: Classification is what defines the process — a trade name alone does not establish sensitivity level or removal method, cannot be checked against QPL-AMS-2644, and "as available" surrenders control of a process variable. Trade names may appear in addition to the classification, never instead of it. An SDS is a safety document, not a process control.

steps.prompt: Processing section: "Spray-rinse at 60 psi with 120 °F water to speed throughput, then dry at 175 °F until the next basket is ready."
options: In-limits — wash and dryer settings are shop preference
Only the dryer temperature needs to change
Both fail the arithmetic audit — E1417 caps the spray rinse at 40 psi with 50-100 °F water and the dryer at 160 °F, and "until the next basket" is not a time control
Acceptable if the penetrant dwell is doubled to compensate
steps.feedback: Hot, high-pressure washing strips penetrant out of the very flaws you are paid to find, and an over-temperature dryer bakes out what survives — which is exactly why the practice caps them at 40 psi, 50-100 °F, and 160 °F. Numbers outside the governing limits fail completeness review unless a documented qualification accompanies them, and no dwell change "compensates" for either.

steps.prompt: Evaluation section, complete text: "Evaluate all indications to applicable standards."
options: Deficient — the procedure must identify the specific acceptance document and revision, or state the limits, so accept / reject is determinable and repeatable
Acceptable — every certified Level II already knows the applicable standards
Acceptable if QA countersigns each report
A problem only for visible-dye work, since fluorescent indications are unambiguous
steps.feedback: Acceptance criteria are a minimum-content element. "Applicable standards" names no document, no revision, and no limits — two competent inspectors could reach different dispositions of the same indication. Personal knowledge and countersignatures do not substitute for stated criteria, and the deficiency has nothing to do with penetrant type.

steps.prompt: Revised draft: classifications complete, parameters in-limits, acceptance criteria cited by document and revision. The signature block reads "Prepared and released: A. Reyes, PT Level II." Production asks you to start running parts.
options: Run parts — the author is certified and the content is now correct
Hold — a PT procedure requires approval by a certified PT Level III before use, plus submittal to the cognizant engineering organization when the contract requires it
Hold — every PT procedure must be approved directly by the FAA
Run parts, but have a Level I witness the first lot
steps.feedback: Content can be perfect and the document still not be a usable procedure. E1417 requires Level III approval, and NAS 410 makes that approval a defined Level III responsibility; CEO approval applies when the contract invokes it — not "the FAA directly," and not never. A Level I witness adds nothing, because the missing element is approval authority, not extra eyes.

checkQuestions.question: Under ASTM E1417, what approval must a penetrant procedure carry before it is used?
options: Sign-off by the inspector who will run it
Approval by the penetrant materials supplier
Approval by an individual certified as Level III in PT — plus the cognizant engineering organization when the contract requires it
A notarized signature from the quality manager
checkQuestions.explanation: E1417 requires written procedures to be approved by a certified PT Level III, and to be submitted to the cognizant engineering organization for approval when the contract invokes that requirement. Authorship, supply, or a quality-manager signature do not substitute for Level III technical approval.

checkQuestions.question: A draft identifies its penetrant system only as "Brand X penetrant with Brand Y remover." Why does this fail a completeness review?
options: Without the AMS 2644 classification — Type, Method, sensitivity level, developer form, remover class — the procedure does not define the process and cannot be verified against the QPL
Trade names are proprietary and are forbidden in procedures
Brand-name products are not permitted on aerospace hardware
The remover in a written procedure must always be water
checkQuestions.explanation: The classification is the process definition: it fixes fluorescent versus visible, the removal method, the sensitivity level, and the developer form, and it lets anyone verify the chemicals against QPL-AMS-2644. Trade names may appear in addition to the classification, but never in place of it.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of process parameters in a draft would pass a check against ASTM E1417 limits?
options: Dryer holding parts at 180 °F to cut cycle time
Manual spray rinse at 60 psi
Hydrophilic immersion remover at 40 % concentration
70 °F rinse water at 35 psi, with the dryer holding parts at 150 °F
checkQuestions.explanation: E1417 caps the spray rinse at 40 psi with 50-100 °F water, hydrophilic immersion concentration at 35 % by volume, and dryer part temperature at 160 °F. Only the last option sits inside every limit; each of the others exceeds one.

checkQuestions.question: A draft procedure passes the completeness check. What does the workability review add?
options: It verifies the page numbering and formatting
It confirms the shop can execute the procedure as written — equipment holds the numbers, the part fits and drains, lighting and QPL materials are on hand, and the timing fits the line
It replaces the requirement for Level III approval
It confirms the parts contain no discontinuities
checkQuestions.explanation: Completeness asks whether every required element is present and in-limits on paper; workability asks whether this shop, with its tanks, ovens, booth, materials, and schedule, can actually run the procedure exactly as written. A complete but unworkable draft goes back for revision, never gets improvised around.

checkQuestions.question: Why must a procedure state maximums — not just minimums — for times such as penetrant dwell and development?
options: Because the process fails in both directions: too short starves penetrant entry or bleed-out, while too long lets penetrant dry in flaws or spreads indications into fuzzy blobs that cannot be evaluated honestly
Because maximums exist only to shorten the work shift
Because OSHA sets the maximum times for penetrant processing
Because minimum times apply only to visible-dye penetrants
checkQuestions.explanation: Time limits bound the process on both sides: dwell needs at least 10 minutes for capillary entry but must not be left until the penetrant dries, and development needs at least 10 minutes for bleed-out but over-development degrades indication shape and sizing. A draft listing only minimums is incomplete.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §4.1 (inspection procedures — minimum requirements)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (written procedure content, approval, and process limits)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (T-621 written procedure requirements; essential and nonessential variables)
NAS 410, NDT personnel certification (Level III procedure-approval responsibility)
